examples.title: Discretionary Access Control (DAC) - Simple File Permission System
examples.description: Demonstrates DAC where resource owners can grant or revoke access to other users. This is similar to Unix file permissions where the owner controls access.
examples.explanation: This DAC implementation shows: 1) Owner (Alice) creates a resource, 2) Owner explicitly grants permissions to other users, 3) Each user has a list of allowed access types, 4) Access is checked by looking up user's permissions. This mimics real-world file systems where owners control who can access their files.

examples.title: Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) - Company System
examples.description: Demonstrates RBAC where access is granted based on user roles rather than individual permissions. Users are assigned roles, and roles have specific permissions.
examples.code:
from enum import Enum

class Role(Enum):
    ADMIN = "admin"
    MANAGER = "manager"
    EMPLOYEE = "employee"
    GUEST = "guest"

class Permission(Enum):
    READ = "read"
    WRITE = "write"
    DELETE = "delete"
    APPROVE = "approve"
    AUDIT = "audit"

# Define role-permission mapping
ROLE_PERMISSIONS = {
    Role.ADMIN: [Permission.READ, Permission.WRITE, Permission.DELETE, Permission.APPROVE, Permission.AUDIT],
    Role.MANAGER: [Permission.READ, Permission.WRITE, Permission.APPROVE],
    Role.EMPLOYEE: [Permission.READ, Permission.WRITE],
    Role.GUEST: [Permission.READ]
}

class User:
    def __init__(self, username, role):
        self.username = username
        self.role = role
    
    def has_permission(self, permission):
        """Check if user's role has the required permission"""
        return permission in ROLE_PERMISSIONS.get(self.role, [])
    
    def __str__(self):
        return f"{self.username} ({self.role.value})"

class Resource:
    def __init__(self, name, min_role):
        self.name = name
        self.min_role = min_role  # Minimum role required to access
    
    def access(self, user, permission):
        """Attempt to access resource with given permission"""
        # Check role hierarchy
        role_hierarchy = {Role.GUEST: 0, Role.EMPLOYEE: 1, Role.MANAGER: 2, Role.ADMIN: 3}
        
        if role_hierarchy[user.role] < role_hierarchy[self.min_role]:
            return False
        
        # Check specific permission
        return user.has_permission(permission)

# Demo
admin = User("Alice", Role.ADMIN)
manager = User("Bob", Role.MANAGER)
employee = User("Charlie", Role.EMPLOYEE)
guest = User("Diana", Role.GUEST)

# Define resources with minimum required roles
confidential_file = Resource("Financial_Report.pdf", Role.MANAGER)
public_file = Resource("Company_Policy.txt", Role.GUEST)

users = [admin, manager, employee, guest]

print("=== Accessing Financial Report (Manager+) ===")
for user in users:
    result = "GRANTED" if confidential_file.access(user, Permission.READ) else "DENIED"
    print(f"{user}: {result}")

print("\n=== Accessing Company Policy (Guest+) ===")
for user in users:
    result = "GRANTED" if public_file.access(user, Permission.READ) else "DENIED"
    print(f"{user}: {result}")
examples.explanation: RBAC implementation: 1) Define roles (Admin, Manager, Employee, Guest), 2) Assign permissions to each role, 3) Users are assigned roles, 4) Resources have minimum role requirements, 5) Access is granted if user has appropriate role AND specific permission. This is widely used in enterprise systems for simplified permission management.
examples.spaceComplexity: O(r) where r is number of roles

examples.title: Attribute-Based Access Control (ABAC) - Dynamic Access Decision
examples.description: Demonstrates ABAC where access decisions are based on multiple attributes including user attributes, resource attributes, and environmental conditions (time, location, device).
examples.code:
from datetime import datetime, time
from enum import Enum

class Department(Enum):
    HR = "HR"
    IT = "IT"
    FINANCE = "FINANCE"
    SALES = "SALES"

class Location(Enum):
    OFFICE = "OFFICE"
    REMOTE = "REMOTE"
    PARTNER_SITE = "PARTNER_SITE"

class User:
    def __init__(self, name, department, clearance_level, position, location):
        self.name = name
        self.department = department
        self.clearance_level = clearance_level  # 1-5
        self.position = position
        self.location = location

class Resource:
    def __init__(self, name, department_required, min_clearance, restricted_hours=None):
        self.name = name
        self.department_required = department_required
        self.min_clearance = min_clearance
        self.restricted_hours = restricted_hours  # (start, end) tuple

class ABACEngine:
    def __init__(self):
        self.current_time = datetime.now().time()
    
    def evaluate_access(self, user, resource):
        """Evaluate access based on multiple attributes"""
        reasons = []
        
        # Check 1: Department match or user has cross-department clearance
        if user.department != resource.department_required and user.clearance_level < 4:
            reasons.append(f"Department {user.department.value} not authorized for {resource.department_required.value} resource")
            return False, reasons
        
        # Check 2: Clearance level
        if user.clearance_level < resource.min_clearance:
            reasons.append(f"Insufficient clearance: need {resource.min_clearance}, have {user.clearance_level}")
            return False, reasons
        
        # Check 3: Time-based restriction
        if resource.restricted_hours:
            start, end = resource.restricted_hours
            if not (start <= self.current_time <= end):
                reasons.append(f"Access allowed only between {start} and {end}, current: {self.current_time}")
                return False, reasons
        
        # Check 4: Location-based access
        if user.location == Location.PARTNER_SITE and resource.department_required != Department.IT:
            reasons.append("Partner site access limited to IT department only")
            return False, reasons
        
        return True, ["All access policies satisfied"]

# Demo
users = [
    User("Alice", Department.HR, 3, "HR_Manager", Location.OFFICE),
    User("Bob", Department.IT, 5, "Security_Admin", Location.REMOTE),
    User("Charlie", Department.FINANCE, 2, "Accountant", Location.OFFICE),
    User("Diana", Department.SALES, 4, "Sales_Director", Location.PARTNER_SITE),
]

resources = [
    Resource("Employee_Records", Department.HR, 2, (time(9, 0), time(18, 0))),
    Resource("Server_Config", Department.IT, 4, None),
    Resource("Q4_Financials", Department.FINANCE, 3, (time(8, 0), time(20, 0))),
]

engine = ABACEngine()

print("=== ABAC Access Decisions ===\n")
for user in users:
    print(f"User: {user.name} (Dept: {user.department.value}, Clearance: {user.clearance_level}, Location: {user.location.value})")
    for resource in resources:
        granted, reasons = engine.evaluate_access(user, resource)
        status = "✓ GRANTED" if granted else "✗ DENIED"
        print(f"  -> {resource.name}: {status}")
        if not granted:
            print(f"     Reason: {reasons[0]}")
    print()
examples.explanation: ABAC is the most flexible model: 1) Combines user attributes (department, clearance, position), 2) Resource attributes (required department, minimum clearance), 3) Environmental conditions (time, location), 4) Multiple policies are evaluated together. This example shows how real-world systems make complex access decisions based on many factors simultaneously.
